Document Title: A High-Throughput Implementation of the MUSIC Algorithm Using AMD Versal AI Engine
Authors: P. Zhou; B. Berkane; V. Druz; M. Rollins
Author Affiliations: Fidus Systems Inc., Ottawa, Canada; Fidus Systems Inc., Ottawa, Canada; Fidus Systems Inc., Kitchener, Canada; AMD, Ottawa, Canada
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 33rd Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
Abstract: The MUSIC (Multiple Signal Classification) algorithm is a well-established method for Direction of Arrival (DOA) estimation in array signal processing. Its ability to accurately resolve multiple signals arriving from different directions, even when they are closely spaced, makes it indispensable in applications requiring high-precision DOA estimation, such as radar, sonar, and wireless communications. Hardware-based implementations of the MUSIC algorithm have proven to be highly valuable and have attracted significant research interests. The Versal platform represents the latest generation of AMD's FPGA offerings, with the AI Engine emerging as a disruptive innovation due to its massive computational power. The AI Engine is designed to efficiently handle complex computations and features a 2D array of SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) and VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) vector processors, also known as compute tiles. The data processing chain of the MUSIC algorithm consists of several functional blocks: QR Decomposition (QRD), Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), Spectrum Calculation, and DOA Identification. The incoming data to the MUSIC processing chain are organized into batches of snapshots. Each batch is represented as an $M$ × $N$ matrix, where $M$ denotes the number of snapshots and $N$ the number of ULA (Uniform Linear Array) elements. All data within these matrices are stored in complex single-precision floating-point format. For our implementation, $M$ and $N$ are set to 128 and 8 respectively. To fully harness the SIMD/VLIW capabilities of the AI Engine and take advantage of its up to 400 compute tiles, we deploy a deep pipelined architecture that maximizes the throughput of the MUSIC algorithm. This architecture breaks down complex operations into smaller, sequential stages, distributing the workload of DOA estimation across multiple stages. Each pipeline stage is responsible for a specific part of the computation, enabling concurrent processing of multiple data elements in parallel. Additionally, loops in QRD and SVD operations are unrolled and distributed across multiple tiles for parallelism. The AI Engine can be conceptualized as a 2D array of vector processors. To leverage its SIMD/VLIW capabilities, we utilize the data type aie:: vector < cfloat, 8 > as the standard for all operations. This allows for 8-lane SIMD operations on complex numbers in single-precision floats, ensuring both consistency and alignment with the AI Engine's vectorized processing power. To evaluate the throughput performance of our MUSIC implementation, a hardware-in-the-loop setup was deployed. This setup involved a system where a laptop running a MATLAB system model generated batches of snapshot data and sent them to an AMD Versal AI Core Series VCK190 Evaluation Kit [1]. These snapshot data were stored in the VCK190's DDR4 memory and transferred to the AI Engine via DMA. Once received, the AI Engine processed the snapshot data in 128 × 8 matrices using the MUSIC algorithm and produced up to 7 DOA estimates within each batch. These DOA estimates were then written back to the DDR4 memory for further analysis and visualization. After processing all snapshots in a batch, the AI Engine's average processing time was measured using profiling counters. This demonstrated a sustained throughput rate of 1μs per batch of 128 samples for an 8-element ULA. In our implementation, 157 out of the total 400 tiles on the AMD Versal VC1902 device mounted on VCK190 were utilized. In this paper, we presented a high-throughput implementation of the MUSIC algorithm powered by the AMD Versal AI Engine. Our implementation successfully estimated up to 7 directions of signal arrivals simultaneously, achieving a sustained throughput rate of 1μs per batch of 128 snapshots for an 8-element ULA. This result signifies the immense potential of combining advanced signal processing algorithms with state-of-the-art hardware to deliver unprecedented performance in real-time signal processing applications. Our solution has been published with the complete source code available from the AMD GitHub website [2].
Author Keywords: MUSIC Algorithm;Direction of Arrival;QR Decomposition;Singular Value Decomposition;AI Engine;AMD Versal FPGA;VC1902;VCK190

Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing and Communication Technology (SPCT)
Abstract: Root-MUSIC is a classical high-resolution DOA estimation algorithm. However, it is based on strict model assumptions, and its performance degrades when model mismatches occur, such as scenarios involving sparse linear arrays (SLAs), coherent signals, or broadband signals. Its performance further deteriorates with limited snapshots and low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). To address these challenges, we propose the Residual Covariance Reconstruction Network (RCR-Net), a deep learning framework that reconstructs an ideal, full-rank Hermitian-Toeplitz covariance matrix of a virtual uniform linear array (ULA) for Root-MUSIC. By leveraging the differentiability of the Root-MUSIC algorithm, the proposed framework enables end-to-end training and avoids requiring ground truth labels of the virtual covariance matrix for DOA estimation. The proposed method provides continuous, high-precision estimates and avoids the grid mismatch errors of classification-based approaches. A single network architecture can handle diverse scenarios without modification and successfully estimates more sources than physical sensors. Simulations show that the proposed method outperforms other sparse reconstruction methods and existing deep learning methods in challenging scenarios.

Abstract: The task of extracting the dominant pitch from polyphonic audio is crucial in the music information retrieval field. To boost the performance, the models are adapted on minimal labeled data from the target domain, a method known as the supervised domain adaptation. Document Title: Comparing Deep Models and Evaluation Strategies for Multi-Pitch Estimation in Music Recordings
Authors: C. Weiß; G. Peeters
Author Affiliations: LTCI, Télécom Paris, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Palaiseau, France; LTCI, Télécom Paris, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Palaiseau, France
Publication Title: IEEE/ACM Transactions on Audio, Speech, and Language Processing
Abstract: Extracting pitch information from music recordings is a challenging but important problem in music signal processing. Frame-wise transcription or multi-pitch estimation aims for detecting the simultaneous activity of pitches in polyphonic music recordings and has recently seen major improvements thanks to deep-learning techniques, with a variety of proposed model architectures. In this paper, we compare different architectures based on convolutional neural networks, the U-net structure, and self-attention components. We propose several modifications to these architectures including self-attention modules for skip connections, recurrent layers to replace the self-attention, and a multi-task strategy with simultaneous prediction of the degree of polyphony. We compare variants of these architectures in different sizes for multi-pitch estimation, focusing on Western classical music beyond the piano-solo scenario using the MusicNet and Schubert Winterreise datasets. Our experiments indicate that most architectures yield competitive results and that larger model variants seem to be beneficial. However, we find that these results substantially depend on randomization effects and the particular choice of the training–test split, which questions the claim of superiority for particular architectures given only small improvements. We therefore investigate the influence of dataset splits in the presence of several movements of a work cycle (cross-version evaluation) and propose a best-practice evaluation strategy for MusicNet, which weakens the influence of individual test tracks and suppresses overfitting to specific works and recording conditions. A final cross-dataset evaluation suggests that improvements on one specific dataset do not necessarily generalize to other scenarios, thus emphasizing the need for further high-quality multi-pitch datasets in order to reliably measure progress in music transcription tasks.

This paper contributes to this field by introducing a new dataset specifically tailored for the analysis of EOTC chants, also known as Yaredawi Zema. This work provides a comprehensive overview of a 10 hours of dataset, 369 instances, creation and curation process, including rigorous quality assurance measures. Our dataset has a detailed word-level temporal boundary and reading tone annotation along with the corresponding chanting mode label of audios. Moreover, we have also identified the chanting options associated with multiple chanting notations in the manuscript by annotating them accordingly. Our goal in making this dataset available to the public1 is to encourage more research and study of EOTC chants, including lyrics transcription, lyric-to-audio alignment, and music generation tasks. Abstract: Despite the release of SongEval, spanning over 2,000 tracks, effective and generalizable song aesthetics evaluation remains a formidable challenge. Hence, a holistic representation that seamlessly integrates lyrical, acoustic, and high-level semantic cues is essential for reliable song aesthetics evaluation. Second, music appreciation is inherently subjective, necessitating diverse and large-scale annotations that are prohibitively costly. With only 2k+ labeled songs, the task reduces to a few-shot regime, imposing a severe generalization burden. To tackle these challenges, we leverage tens of millions of tracks from Netease Cloud Music to pre-train two music representation models from different perspectives, yielding holistic representations for accurate aesthetics scoring. Further, inspired by meta-learning, we devise a metric-based few-shot learner that fully exploits SongEval’s limited annotations, thereby enhancing the few-shot performance and generalization ability. Experimental results on the validation set1 confirm the effectiveness of our method. Moreover, we have publicly released the pre-trained music representation models and code2 3 to facilitate further research on song aesthetics evaluation.

Author Affiliations: Conservatory of Music, Jiangxi University of Applied Science, Nanchang, China; Art Troupe, Nanchang Children’s Palace, Nanchang, China
Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: This paper introduces an optimized hybrid deep feature fusion framework designed for hardware-efficient music retrieval, specifically architected for heterogeneous AMD Xilinx FPGA platforms. To overcome the computational bottlenecks of probabilistic indexing in large-scale retrieval, the framework integrates three specialized hardware-accelerated engines, orchestrated via asynchronous ping-pong buffering and cycle-accurate AXI4-Stream scheduling to seamlessly synchronize dataflow across the PL and AIE domains: 1) a pipelined hybrid feature fusion module synthesized via Vivado HLS, which dynamically merges deep convolutional embeddings with statistical spectral descriptors to generate robust acoustic signatures, achieving a 93.8% retrieval precision with a processing latency of only 2.1 ms for 30-second audio segments; 2) an AI Engine–accelerated Variational Inference Processor that executes complex logistic normal approximations required by Correlated Topic Models (CTM) over an AXI4-Stream interconnect, optimizing covariance matrix operations to reduce BRAM utilization by 38% compared to standard architectures while sustaining a throughput of 480 audio segments per second; and 3) a DSP-optimized Topic Likelihood Estimator utilizing DSP48E2 slices to parallelize posterior probability calculations for rapid similarity matching, reducing inference latency by  $5.6\times $  relative to software-based Gibbs sampling and capping total power consumption at 14 W. Validated on three benchmark music datasets (GTZAN, FMA-Small, and MagnaTagATune), the framework demonstrates a 91.5% Top-1 retrieval accuracy, outperforming baseline CNN-only approaches by 12.7% and achieving a 45% reduction in prediction variance across diverse musical textures. The system delivers  $431\times $  and  $20\times $  higher energy efficiency (29 mJ per inference) than equivalent high-performance CPU- and GPU-based solutions, respectively, along with a  $48\times $  smaller memory footprint (8.1 MB), facilitating deployment in edge-based music discovery systems. The complete hardware-software co-design has been verified on Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC devices using the Vitis 2022.2 toolchain.
Author Keywords: Music retrieval;hybrid deep feature fusion;correlated topic model (CTM);hardware-efficient acceleration;FPGA;variational inference;edge computing

Author Affiliations: The Experimental High School Attached to Beijing Normal University, Beijing, China
Publication Title: 2023 IEEE 6th International Conference on Information Systems and Computer Aided Education (ICISCAE)
Abstract: Music widely exists in our daily life. To recognize the music category effectively, the topic namely music classification is to classify the music into various types, such as blues, jazz, pop, etc. With the development of machine learning, music classification using machine learning method has dominated with satisfying performance and real-time speed. In this paper, we aim to analyze the performance of several machine learning methods, including decision tree, random forest, logistic regression and the popular convolution neural network. We firstly preprocess the dataset and convert the music into spectrogram, which is sent to each model. We evaluate those models with mean and standard deviation of correlation rate. We conclude that the convolution neural network obtains the best result, with 75.3% mean correlation rate. This paper provides a brief analysis on music classification using machine learning methods, which can further influence the algorithm design.

Abstract: The Musical Metaverse pilot project is a multidisciplinary research initiative funded under the Italian PRIN 2022 program, aiming to develop an inclusive, low-latency eXtended Reality platform for networked musical collaboration. Authors: M. Perez; H. Kirchhoff; P. Grosche; X. Serra
Author Affiliations: Universitat Pompeu Fabra, Barcelona, Spain; Huawei Munich Research Center, Munich, Germany; Huawei Munich Research Center, Munich, Germany; Universitat Pompeu Fabra, Barcelona, Spain
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing Workshops (ICASSPW)
Abstract: Singing voice transcription is a key task in Music Information Retrieval (MIR) that focuses on identifying sung notes within a music audio segment. Advancing state-of-the-art methods in this area relies heavily on high-quality data, yet annotating such data is resource-intensive and requires musical expertise. In genres like pop music, data sharing is further complicated by copyright and distribution limitations. In this paper, we refine a recently proposed data augmentation technique that leverages AI-generated music audio to address these data-related challenges. Specifically, we create musical accompaniments for vocals with known target notes, enabling the generation of new mixes that retain the original piece’s harmony while introducing substantial audio variation. Our cross-dataset experiments reveal that using harmony-matched mixes improves generalization, though performance remains below that achieved by training with additional real data.

Abstract: Automatic music classification algorithms have become a research hotspot in the field of music information retrieval. This study uses deep learning to denoise and normalize audio signals, and then uses Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCC) and image texture, color and other feature extraction techniques to establish rich feature representations for music works. Abstract: Indian classical dance such as Kathakali is composed of complex hand gestures, body moments, facial expressions and background music. Due to the complexities involved in its hand-gesture language, it is often difficult to understand kathakali mudras. In this paper, we generated a dataset for Kathakali hand gestures and explore ways to recognize Kathakali dance mudras performed by artistes with the help of machine learning and deep learning techniques. There are 24 classes of hand gestures that are used to convey the story by the performer in Kathakali. We proposed a Support Vector Machine (SVM) model and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model which classify the images into 24 different classes. We compared the performance of machine learning algorithms and deep learning algorithms. Our results show that deep learning algorithms gave up to 74% accuracy. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to generate a dataset of Kathakali hand gestures, explore data pre-processing techniques for machine learning techniques and applying deep learning techniques for classification of Kathakali hand gestures.

The music signals in this work are first transformed into the relevant spectrograms. The classifier then receives these spectrogram features as input. Abstract: In recent years, AI-based music generation and transformation technologies have received widespread attention. This study presents an automated system for transcribing vocal humming audio into Guzheng audio, integrating the YIN algorithm and genetic algorithm, and optimizing pitch and timbre matching through pitch-lowering processing. First, the YIN algorithm is used to extract the fundamental frequency of the humming audio, and then the genetic algorithm optimizes the frequency sequence to better align with the Guzheng's pitch range. To further improve the audio generation quality, the system introduces pitch-lowering processing, adjusting the generated audio frequencies to fit within the Guzheng's pitch range. Experimental results show that pitch-lowering processing significantly enhances the accuracy of the pitch and timbre matching. Moreover, the genetic algorithm outperforms the YIN algorithm in optimizing pitch and timbre, with the generated audio exhibiting a more realistic Guzheng timbre. Ultimately, the system achieves significant improvements in pitch, timbre, and auditory effects. The proposed transcription scheme demonstrates good applicability in musical applications involving pitch range conversion and provides new research insights for automated instrument transcription.

Document Title: Tuning Immersion and Performance with Adaptive Generative Music in VR
Authors: J. Wen; D. Giunchi; P. Cascarano; R. Bovo; E. Ofek; A. Steed
Author Affiliations: University College London, U.K.; University of Birmingham, U.K.; University of Bologna, Italy; University of Greenwich, U.K.; University of Birmingham, U.K.; University College London, U.K.
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
Abstract: Music in virtual environments has long been treated as a temporal evolving element, enhancing atmosphere and game pace but rarely considered as a performance adaptive element. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and procedural audio make it possible to generate music that adapts in real time to player actions and system state. Yet, despite its potential, the behavioural impact of such adaptive generative soundtracks in head-mounted display-based virtual reality (VR) remains largely unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce a VR archery system that integrates Google MusicFXDJ with Ubiq-Genie to deliver continuous AI-generated adaptive music driven by gameplay events. In a within-subjects experiment (N = 22), participants completed trials with either a stylistically-matched fixed soundtrack or an adaptive soundtrack that escalated tension across four phases as arrows depleted. Measures combined self-reported ratings of presence, focus, stress, and emotional impact, with performance metrics of accuracy and aiming time. Results reveal that adaptive generative music not only heightens immersion and emotional salience but also modulates motor precision in an arousal-dependent inverted-U pattern: moderate musical tension improved accuracy and speed, whereas excessive tension impaired them. These findings establish AI-generated music as a powerful behavioural feedback modality in VR, opening pathways for training, rehabilitation, and next-generation immersive entertainment.
Author Keywords: Virtual Reality;Adaptive Music;Generative Audio;Presence;Arousal;Performance;User Study

Author Affiliations: Acoustics Research Centre, University of Salford, Salford, U.K.; Hearing Sciences, School of Medicine, University of Nottingham, Nottingham, U.K.; School of Music, University of Leeds, Leeds, U.K.; School of Computer Science, University of Sheffield, Sheffield, U.K.; Acoustics Research Centre, University of Salford, Salford, U.K.; Acoustics Research Centre, University of Salford, Salford, U.K.; Hearing Sciences, School of Medicine, University of Nottingham, Nottingham, U.K.; Acoustics Research Centre, University of Salford, Salford, U.K.; School of Music, University of Leeds, Leeds, U.K.; Acoustics Research Centre, University of Salford, Salford, U.K.; Hearing Sciences, School of Medicine, University of Nottingham, Nottingham, U.K.
Publication Title: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
Abstract: Listening to music can be an issue for those with a hearing impairment, and hearing aids are not a universal solution. This paper details the first use of an open challenge methodology to improve the audio quality of music for those with hearing loss through machine learning. The first challenge (CAD1) had 9 participants. The second was a 2024 ICASSP grand challenge (ICASSP24), which attracted 17 entrants. The challenge tasks concerned demixing and remixing pop/rock music to allow a personalized rebalancing of the instruments in the mix, along with amplification to correct for raised hearing thresholds. The software baselines provided for entrants to build upon used two state-of-the-art demix algorithms: Hybrid Demucs and Open-Unmix. Objective evaluation used HAAQI, the Hearing-Aid Audio Quality Index. No entries improved on the best baseline in CAD1. It is suggested that this arose because demixing algorithms are relatively mature, and recent work has shown that access to large (private) datasets is needed to further improve performance. Learning from this, for ICASSP24 the scenario was made more difficult by using loudspeaker reproduction and specifying gains to be applied before remixing. This also made the scenario more useful for listening through hearing aids. Nine entrants scored better than the best ICASSP24 baseline. Most of the entrants used a refined version of Hybrid Demucs and NAL-R amplification. The highest scoring system combined the outputs of several demixing algorithms in an ensemble approach. These challenges are now open benchmarks for future research with freely available software and data.

Document Title: Graph-Based Attentive Sequential Model With Metadata for Music Recommendation
Authors: H. Weng; J. Chen; D. Wang; X. Zhang; D. Yu
Author Affiliations: School of Computer Science and Technology, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, China; School of Computer Science and Technology, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, China; School of Computer Science and Technology, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, China; School of Computer Science and Technology, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, China; School of Computer Science and Technology, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, China
Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: Massive music data and diverse listening behaviors have caused great difficulties for existing methods in user-personalized recommendation scenarios. Most previous music recommendation models extract features from temporal relationships among sequential listening records and ignore the utilization of additional information, such as music’s singer and album. Especially, a piece of music is commonly created by a specific musician and belongs to a particular album. Singer and album information, regarded as music metadata, can be utilized as important auxiliary information among different music pieces and may considerably influence the user’s choices of music. In this paper, we focus on the music sequential recommendation task with the consideration of the additional information and propose a novel Graph-based Attentive Sequential model with Metadata (GASM), which incorporates metadata to enrich music representations and effectively mine the user’s listening behavior patterns. Specifically, we first use a directed listening graph to model the relations between various kinds of nodes (user, music, singer, album) and then adopt the graph neural networks to learn their latent representation vectors. After that, we decompose the user’s preference for music into long-term, short-term and dynamic components with personalized attention networks. Finally, GASM integrates three types of preferences to predict the next (new) music in accordance with the user’s music taste. Extensive experiments have been conducted on three real-world datasets, and the results show that the proposed method GASM achieves better performance than baselines.

Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: We present a novel timbre transfer model that uses an enhanced diffusion architecture to convert music from various instruments into Erhu timbre. The Erhu, a traditional Chinese instrument, is difficult to simulate due to its rich vibrato and smooth note transitions. Existing Musical Instrument Digital Interface systems struggle to capture its nuanced dynamics. Our model integrates a Pitch Encoder, a Loudness Encoder, and a Diffusion Decoder. The encoders extract pitch features and dynamic loudness variations, guiding the decoder in generating realistic Erhu timbre. By extracting general musical features, the system generalizes to unseen input types without retraining. Evaluations based on pitch accuracy, cosine similarity, and Fréchet Audio Distance show that our model achieves 96% pitch accuracy and high fidelity in Erhu timbre reproduction. This study demonstrates the potential of diffusion-based timbre transfer models in music generation and provides new directions for future work on both music generation and timbre transfer.

Authors: T. -Y. Chen; Y. -J. Joung

Abstract: Piano cover generation aims to automatically transform a pop song into a piano arrangement. While numerous deep learning approaches have been proposed, existing models often fail to maintain structural consistency with the original song, likely due to the absence of beat-aware mechanisms or the difficulty of modeling complex rhythmic patterns. Rhythmic information is crucial, as it defines structural similarity (e.g., tempo, BPM) and directly impacts the overall quality of the generated music. In this paper, we introduce Etude, a three-stage architecture consisting of Extract, strucTUralize, and DEcode stages. By pre-extracting rhythmic information and applying a novel, simplified REMI-based tokenization, our model produces covers that preserve proper song structure, enhance fluency and musical dynamics, and support highly controllable generation through style injection. Subjective evaluations with human listeners show that Etude substantially outperforms prior models, achieving a quality level comparable to that of human composers.

Publication Title: 2026 IEEE/SICE International Symposium on System Integration (SII)
Abstract: To facilitate efficient laparoscopic surgical education, a system was developed that utilizes machine learning to classify surgical skill levels—novice, intermediate, and expert—based on the motion dynamics of surgical instruments. This system not only categorizes surgical proficiency but also incorporates SHAP, an Explainable AI technique, to provide insights into the rationale behind each classification result. For the machine learning dataset, the movements of four surgical instruments were recorded using a motion capture (mocap) system during total nephrectomy training sessions conducted on 46 cadaveric specimens prepared for laparoscopic surgery. The entire nephrectomy procedure was divided into three distinct processes: colon mobilization (Process 1), renal vascular dissection (Process 2), and incision and removal of the remaining tissues (Process 3). Surgical skill analysis was performed separately for each phase. Surgeons were categorized into three groups based on their prior experience with laparoscopic procedures: novices (0–9 cases), intermediates (10–49 cases), and experts (50 or more cases). A total of 111 features were extracted from the instrument motion data for each phase, and comparative analyses were conducted across the three groups. Multiple machine learning approaches—including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Principal Component Analysis followed by SVM (PCA-SVM), and Random Forest—were employed to develop models for classifying surgeons into three distinct skill levels. The classification performance of these models was subsequently validated. The results revealed that features related to efficiency and speed significantly contributed to differences in surgical skill levels. The developed system enables quantitative comparison and visualization of specific instrument characteristics. This system contributes to intelligent integration of surgical education and Explainable AI, providing actionable feedback for skill improvement.
IEEE Terms: Training;Support vector machines;Deep learning;Minimally invasive surgery;Explainable AI;Instruments;Feature extraction;Motion capture;Space vector modulation;Principal component analysis

Document Title: Design and Development of Music Genre Classification based on Bi-STM-GRNN Model
Authors: Z. Huang
Author Affiliations: College of Music, Henan University, Kaifeng, China
Publication Title: 2025 Third International Conference on Networks, Multimedia and Information Technology (NMITCON)
Abstract: Accurate recognition of music styles plays a critical role in music recommendation systems, content classification, and personalized audio experiences. This paper presents the design and development of a robust music style recognition system utilizing deep learning techniques. The system is built to automatically classify music tracks into various genres or styles based on audio features extracted from raw waveforms and spectrograms. A deep neural architecture combining Gated Recurrent Neural Networks (GRNN) and BiLong Short Term memory (Bi-LSTMs) is proposed to capture both spatial and temporal patterns inherent in musical signals. The model is trained and evaluated using benchmark datasets such as GTZAN and Extended Ballroom, incorporating features like Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs), chroma vectors, and rhythmic structures. To enhance performance and generalizability, techniques such as data augmentation, transfer learning, and dropout regularization are applied. Experimental results demonstrate high classification accuracy (accuracy of $\mathbf{9 8 \%}$), outperforming traditional machine learning methods. The system is further deployed with a user-friendly interface for real-time genre prediction, showcasing its practical applicability in digital music platforms and musicology research.

Authors: E. Manilow; P. Seetharaman; B. Pardo
Author Affiliations: Interactive Audio Lab, Northwestern University, Evanston, IL, USA; Interactive Audio Lab, Northwestern University, Evanston, IL, USA; Interactive Audio Lab, Northwestern University, Evanston, IL, USA
Publication Title: ICASSP 2020 - 2020 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
Abstract: We present a single deep learning architecture that can both separate an audio recording of a musical mixture into constituent single-instrument recordings and transcribe these instruments into a human-readable format at the same time, learning a shared musical representation for both tasks. This novel architecture, which we call Cerberus, builds on the Chimera network for source separation by adding a third "head" for transcription. By training each head with different losses, we are able to jointly learn how to separate and transcribe up to five instruments with a single network. We show that separation and transcription are highly complementary with one another and when learned jointly, lead to Cerberus networks that are better at both separation and transcription and generalize better to unseen mixtures.

This paper explores the forecasting of voltage, current, and frequency in power systems. Two main approaches to forecasting in power systems are compared: the statistical approach and the machine learning (ML) approach. The accuracy of both approaches is evaluated simulating measurements obtained from instrument transformers (ITs). Abstract: This research designs and implementation of a music genre classification system that leverages an innovative hybrid approach combining Convolutional Neural Networks, Long Short-Term Memory networks, and an attention mechanism. The CNN component excels at extracting abstract features from audio's mel-frequency cepstral coefficients through a series of convolutional and pooling layers, while the LSTM network adeptly handles long-range dependencies within the sequential audio features. Abstract: Timber is a major attribute of musical sound perception and an important aspect of the hearing evaluation. With the development of brain science and neuroscience, how to use electroencephalogram (EEG) signals to identify musical instrument timbers raises concerns. In this paper, we studied the classification of EEG signals evoked by musical instrument timber based on deep learning methods. First, an oddball paradigm was designed to collect the auditory EEG signals when musical instrument sounds (piano, violin and trumpet) were played to a normal hearing (NH) subject. Then the spatial and temporal characteristics of the EEG data were analysed and the event-related potentials (ERPs) could be found after about 400 ms. Based on these features, four deep learning models were used to classify the EEG data. The experimental results showed that the two neural network models with spatiotemporal decoding structures had better performance than other two models, and their classification accuracies were all up 80% above. It is concluded that the design of musical instrument sounds-evoked EEG experimental paradigm is reasonable, and the proposed deep learning methods can be used objectively to evaluate the hearing of the subject for musical instrument timber.

Abstract: We present a novel approach for affective multimedia content analysis to study how the human keypoints contribute to the perceived emotion of art music. Traditional music information retrieval methodologies have extensively used the cross-modal bias of audio and visual modalities to assess affective states. In the case of art music videos, the visual modality is limited by orchestra footage or static images, lacking the dynamic visual elements commonly found in videos of other music genres. In this paper, we introduce ARTMV, an art music video dataset consisting of perceived static categorical valence labels, music tracks and related dance videos. To overcome the restrictive visual content, our proposed network competitively replaces the visual modality of the videos with the proprioception of the performers from the dance performances of the corresponding art music.

Document Title: Deep Learning-Driven Recommendation Framework for Personalized College Vocal Music Education
Authors: Y. Fang
Author Affiliations: Harbin Preschool Teacher College, Harbin, China
Publication Title: 2025 2nd International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology (SSITCON)
Abstract: In recent years, Vocal music recommendation systems have developed crucial components of digital learning platforms that supports the personalized content delivery in educational settings. However, traditional models significantly depend on user listening history often fail to deliver accurate recommendations for new users leads to cold-start limitations. Hence, this research proposes an enhanced hybrid recommendation framework that incorporates the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to overcome these challenges. Initially, user behavioral data were gathered from public music datasets in the form of play counts and audio content. Then, the behavioral data were normalized and converted into a user–music interaction matrix, while audio signals were preprocessed into Log-Mel spectrograms. After that, the processed data were fed into CNN model to extract high-level acoustic features from the spectrograms for creating the compact content embeddings for each song. Further, a VAE-based preference modeling approach was employed to learn latent user distributions and make initial preference vectors for new users without interaction history. Finally, the proposed CNN–VAE framework produced top-n recommendations by matching audio content features with created user embeddings. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed CNN–VAE framework attained greater performance in terms of accuracy of 0.94% compared to existing CNN model.
Author Keywords: convolutional neural network;vocal music recommendation system;log-mel spectrograms;user–music interaction matrix;variational autoencoder

Publication Title: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
Abstract: The swift proliferation of encrypted network communication has rendered conventional deep packet inspection (DPI) methodologies unsuitable for traffic identification and security surveillance. This study proposes an ensemble machine learning framework for classifying encrypted HTTPS data based on flow-based criteria that protect user privacy. The experimental dataset comprises statistical attributes, including packet size distribution, flow duration, and inter-arrival durations, gathered from actual backbone networks. We employ these attributes to sort network traffic into six application types: live video streaming, video player, music player, file upload, file download, and website browsing. We examine the performance of Support Vector Machine (SVM), Logistic Regression (LR), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Random Forest (RF), and XGBoost. To improve the accuracy and reliability of classification, an ensemble model is created that utilizes the best features of each individual learner. The ensemble method outperforms individual classifiers in recognizing encrypted HTTPS traffic patterns, as evidenced by experimental findings that demonstrate higher accuracy, precision, and F1-score. The suggested framework is a smart, scalable, and privacy-preserving way to analyse current network data. It makes it easier to optimise networks and find intrusions in encrypted settings.

Abstract: With recent advances in artificial intelligence, recurrent neural networks have successfully generated pleasing melodies; however, they have struggled to create a full song that has structure, theme, and uniqueness. Author Affiliations: Dept. of Electronics and Communication, Vidyavardhaka College of Engineering, Mysuru, India; Dept. of Electronics and Communication, Vidyavardhaka College of Engineering, Mysuru, India; Dept. of Electronics and Communication, Vidyavardhaka College of Engineering, Mysuru, India; Dept. of Electronics and Communication, Vidyavardhaka College of Engineering, Mysuru, India; Dept. of Electronics and Communication, Vidyavardhaka College of Engineering, Mysuru, India
Publication Title: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
Abstract: The paradigm of Music Recommendation Systems (MRS) is currently undergoing a fundamental shift, transitioning from static, history-based preference models to context-aware architecture driven by the user’s real-time affective state. While traditional collaborative filtering successfully captures long-term taste, it often fails to address the immediate, situational emotional needs of the listener. This paper presents a comprehensive survey and a proposed architectural framework for an Emotion-Aware Music Recommendation System (EAMRS) optimized for resource-constrained edge environments. This paper perform an in-depth analysis of current methods used in three important areas: (1) Facial Emotion Recognition (FER), where comparison of heavy models like VGG16 with lightweight ones like MobileNetV2 is done; (2) Music Mood Classification (MMC), where judgement of the performance of spectral feature engineering is made and that of deep learning hybrids working end-to-end; and (3) Psychological Emotion Mapping, where The subtle differences are highlighted between mood congruence and mood regulation. Besides, this paper elaborate on the implementation strategy which makes use of depth wise separable convolutions to get real-time inference (approx. 25ms) on standard hardware, and on the CNN-GRU hybrid model for audio analysis that has a classification accuracy of 98% which is quite high. The paper concludes by addressing the "Semantic Gap" in audio analysis and outlining future directions for multimodal fusion.
Author Keywords: Emotion-aware systems;Real-time recommendation;MobileNetV2;Facial Emotion Recognition;Music In- formation Retrieval;Depth wise separable convolutions;Edge Computing;CNN-GRU
IEEE Terms: Modeling;Multiple signal classification;Music;Printing;Mood;Recommender systems;Convolutional neural networks;Timing;Deep learning;Emotion recognition

Document Title: Chromagram Features Analysis for Learning-Based Query by Humming Systems
Authors: K. -Y. Chen; J. -J. Ding
Author Affiliations: National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan; National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Electronics, Information, and Communication (ICEIC)
Abstract: The Query by Humming (QBH) system is a melody-based searching system that can retrieve the song without using the information of the title, the composer, or lyrics. To well distinguish the songs with similar melodies, an accurate QBH system is required. Three primary techniques dominate the performance of audio analysis: (i) onset detection and segmentation, (ii) fundamental frequency analysis, and (iii) melody matching. In this study, we mark a departure from conventional approaches by adopting the Chromagram as the primary feature, coupled with the integration of deep learning techniques to enhance the accuracy of QBH. In music analysis, the Chromagram is served as a pivotal representation of the pitch content of an audio signal over time, facilitating the breakdown of the signal into individual pitch classes. Each pitch class is typically represented as a vector element, encompassing notes such as $\mathrm{C}$, C#, and D. This representation is helpful for chord recognition, key detection, melody extraction, and music similarity comparison. The Chromagram can efficiently capture the harmonic content of a music signal and effectively improve the performance of music retrieval and analysis.
Author Keywords: query by humming (QBH);deep learning;ResNet;music information retrieval;chromagram;constant-Q transform
IEEE Terms: Deep learning;Accuracy;Filtering;Transforms;Harmonic analysis;Feature extraction;Vectors;Multiple signal classification;Power harmonic filters;Optimization

Publication Title: 2025 IEEE XVII International Scientific and Technical Conference on Actual Problems of Electronic Instrument Engineering (APEIE)
Abstract: Dynamic systems are fundamental in describing and analyzing time-evolving processes across diverse domains including energy management, transportation networks, and economic systems. Accurate forecasting of such systems is critical for effective decision-making, strategic planning, and resource optimization. The exponential growth of Big Data technologies has enabled the collection of large-scale, high-dimensional datasets from various sources, while machine learning algorithms have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in pattern recognition and predictive modeling. This study presents an innovative mathematical modelling framework that synergistically integrates Big Data analytics with machine learning techniques for enhanced dynamic system forecasting. The proposed hybrid methodology combines traditional mathematical models based on differential equations and state-space representations with advanced data-driven approaches to capture both structural dynamics and complex nonlinear behaviors. The framework leverages Long Short-Term Memory networks to model temporal dependencies and employs distributed computing platforms for handling massive datasets efficiently. Experimental validation was conducted using real-world electricity demand data from smart grid systems, demonstrating that the hybrid model achieved significant improvements in forecasting accuracy, reducing the Root Mean Squared Error by up to 21% compared to classical mathematical models and by 12% compared to standalone machine learning approaches. The findings underscore the potential of this integrated methodology for real-time forecasting applications and its broad applicability across multiple domains requiring reliable prediction of complex dynamic systems.

Author Affiliations: Telecommunication Engineering Department, University of Jaen, Linares, Spain; Audio Research Group, Tampere University, Tampere, Finland; Audio Research Group, Tampere University, Tampere, Finland; Audio Research Group, Tampere University, Tampere, Finland; Telecommunication Engineering Department, University of Jaen, Linares, Spain; Telecommunication Engineering Department, University of Jaen, Linares, Spain
Publication Title: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
Abstract: Recent advancements in music source separation have significantly progressed, particularly in isolating vocals, drums, and bass elements from mixed tracks. These developments owe much to the creation and use of large-scale, multitrack datasets dedicated to these specific components. However, the challenge of extracting similarly sounding sources from orchestra recordings has not been extensively explored, largely due to a scarcity of comprehensive and clean (i.e bleed-free) multitrack datasets. In this paper, we introduce a novel multitrack dataset called SynthSOD, developed using a set of simulation techniques to create a realistic, musically motivated, and heterogeneous training set comprising different dynamics, natural tempo changes, styles, and conditions by employing high-quality digital libraries that define virtual instrument sounds for MIDI playback (a.k.a., soundfonts). Moreover, we demonstrate the application of a widely used baseline music separation model trained on our synthesized dataset w.r.t to the well-known EnsembleSet, and evaluate its performance under both synthetic and real-world conditions.

Abstract: The timely and accurate diagnosis of plant diseases is what determines the efficiency of farming and food security in the world. In the situation of tomato cultivation, diseases like tomato spot, wilt, blight, and rot are the ones that cause a lot of damage to the yield and quality. This paper presents a new hybrid model that combines convolutional neural networks (CNN) and random forest (RF) for the effective and accurate classification of tomato diseases. The model uses the CNN deep learning capabilities for feature extraction from photos of the tomato leaves and then utilizes RF robust classification for differentiating between four major disease categories and healthy ones. Our approach includes data collection and preprocessing, design and training of model architecture, and performance evaluation through various metrics. The hybrid model has been rigorously assessed on a dataset containing images of diseased and healthy tomato leaves, all in all yielding an impressive overall accuracy of 97.03%. This framework not only expresses the utility of this model as a trustworthy instrument for agricultural disease management, but it also clearly surpasses other models in the field. The results of the study emphasize the advantage of CNN and RF integration, which confirm higher performance in terms of disease classification accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The success of the hybrid model leads to an exploration of the potential role of artificial intelligence for farmers in agriculture, as it demonstrates a promising way to tackle the existing challenges of plant disease detection and classification. By its high accuracy and stable performance, this model is an important part of creating smart and automated agricultural diagnostic systems, which directly leads to enhanced disease management, increased crop yields, and sustainable agriculture.

Document Title: Improving Music Transcription by Pre-Stacking A U-Net
Authors: F. Pedersoli; G. Tzanetakis; K. M. Yi
Author Affiliations: Department of Computer Science, University of Victoria; Department of Computer Science, University of Victoria; Department of Computer Science, University of Victoria
Publication Title: ICASSP 2020 - 2020 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
Abstract: We propose to pre-stack a U-Net as a way of improving the polyphonic music transcription performance of various baseline Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNS). The U-Net, a network architecture based on skip-connections between layers acts as a transformation network followed by a transcription network. Notably, we do not introduce any additional loss terms specific to the transformation network, but instead, jointly train the entire combined model with the original loss function that was designed for the back-end transcription network. We argue that this U-Net network transforms the input signal into a representation that is more effective for transcription, and thus enables the observed improvements in accuracy. We empirically confirm with several experiments using the MusicNet dataset, that the proposed configuration consistently improves the accuracy of transcription networks. This enhancement cannot be achieved by simply introducing more neurons or more layers to the baseline CNNs. Moreover, we show that using the proposed architecture we can go beyond general music transcription and perform transcription in an instrument-specific fashion. By doing so, the original general transcription performance is also increased.
Author Keywords: Automatic Music Transcription;Deep Learning;Deep Architecture;U-Net
IEEE Terms: Source separation;Instruments;Neurons;Transforms;Network architecture;Task analysis;Speech processing

Document Title: Style-Conditioned Music Generation with Transformer-GANs
Authors: W. Wang; J. Li; Y. Li; X. Xing
Author Affiliations: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; School of Electronic and Information Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; School of Electronic and Information Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; School of Electronic and Information Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China
Publication Title: Frontiers of Information Technology & Electronic Engineering
Abstract: Recently, various algorithms have been developed for generating appealing music. However, the style control in the generation process has been somewhat overlooked. Music style refers to the representative and unique appearance presented by a musical work, and it is one of the most salient qualities of music. In this paper, we propose an innovative music generation algorithm capable of creating a complete musical composition from scratch based on a specified target style. A style-conditioned linear Transformer and a style-conditioned patch discriminator are introduced in the model. The style-conditioned linear Transformer models musical instrument digital interface (MIDI) event sequences and emphasizes the role of style information. Simultaneously, the style-conditioned patch discriminator applies an adversarial learning mechanism with two innovative loss functions to enhance the modeling of music sequences. Moreover, we establish a discriminative metric for the first time, enabling the evaluation of the generated music's consistency concerning music styles. Both objective and subjective evaluations of our experimental results indicate that our method's performance with regard to music production is better than the performances encountered in the case of music production with the use of state-of-the-art methods in available public datasets.

Abstract: We present MusicMorph, an interactive system for the user-centric creation and experience of immersive music videos featuring dancing avatars. Given a user-provided music track, our system automatically generates a corresponding dance skeleton and a virtual scene by analyzing the audio features of the track. Users can then select a preferred avatar, which is subsequently rigged with the generated skeleton and rendered within the generated immersive scene. The resulting music video can be experienced in Virtual Reality (VR), offering users an unconstrained, free-viewpoint perspective of the performance. In addition, users can directly manipulate the dance motion by dragging the avatar's joints to refine poses, or modify the animation by inputting specific action commands. This system enables users to upload their own music, watch the real-time generated music video, and perform edits in VR, offering a handson experience of creating an immersive music video from scratch.

Abstract: Musicological studies on jazz performance analysis commonly require a manual selection and transcription of improvised solo parts, both of which can be time-consuming. In order to expand these studies to larger corpora of jazz recordings, algorithms for automatic content analysis can accelerate these processes. In this study, we aim to detect the presence of predominant music instruments in jazz ensemble recordings. This information can guide a structural analysis in order to detect improvised solo parts. As the main contribution, we perform a comparative study on predominant automatic instrument recognition (AIR) in jazz ensembles using a taxonomy of 11 common instruments including singing voice. We compare the performance of three state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (CNNs) including a recurrent variant and one with an attention mechanism. Our main finding is that while all networks perform comparably, the attention-based model learns the most compact feature representation as it is by orders of magnitude smaller than the other models.

Abstract: This study Rasathura explores the emotional dimensions of Sri Lankan music, specifically Sinhala songs, by developing an emotional classification model using both audio features and lyrical analysis. For audio analysis, JAudio was used to extract musical data, and machine learning (ML) techniques, including Random Forest, Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO), Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, and Logistic Regression, were evaluated for classification. Abstract: In the digital age, streaming platforms have revolutionized how we access and interact with music, highlighting the need for more intuitive ways to organize and categorize our ever-growing music collections. The challenge lies in effectively classifying tracks into similar genres and styles to enhance user experience through improved music discovery and recommendation. In this context, machine learning stands out as a powerful tool. Traditional research in the field focuses on the auditory characteristics of music, such as timbre and rhythm. Nevertheless, the incorporation of spectrogram analysis introduces a richer layer of data representation, capturing the intricate musical textures that distinguish genres. This study proposes a novel approach to music genre classification, leveraging classic machine learning algorithms and the recently proposed contrastive dissimilarity method. Our methodology, which involves a detailed examination of spectrograms and the use of conventional feature extraction methods such as Local Binary Patterns (LBP), Local Phase Quantization (LPQ), Binarized Statistical Image Features (BSIF), and Oriented Basic Image Features (OBIF), combined with deep neural embeddings estimated using the contrastive dissimilarity method, offers a more comprehensive and accurate way to classify music genres. Our comparative analysis, conducted on three benchmark music genre datasets - GTZAN, Latin Music Database, and ISMIR 2004 - demonstrates promising results that approach the performance of current state-of-the-art methods.

Document Title: HAMLET-Net: A Deep Harmonic-Aware Semisupervised Network for Interpretable Melody Extraction of Music and Singing
Authors: W. Wang
Author Affiliations: School of Education, Jilin International Studies University, Changchun, China
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Communication, Computer, and Information Technology (IC3IT)
Abstract: Melody extraction from polyphonic music and singing is a basic but difficult task in Music Information Retrieval (MIR) due to issues such as overlapping harmonics, various timbral presentational conditions, and limited labeled data. Thus, this study presents Harmonic-Aware Multi-level Learning and Extraction Transformer-Network (HAMLETNet), which is a deep semi-supervised framework to extract melody. Furthermore, the proposed HAMLET-Net employs harmonic-aware, self-supervised pre-training for pitchinvariant features, multi-level transformer encoding to model melodic dependencies on micro and macro levels. Specifically, probabilistic semi-supervised learning to manage uncertainty, and CRF-based decoding procedures to guarantee temporal and harmonic coherence. Moreover, pitch, note labels, onsets, offsets, and voicing jointly predicts through a multi-task learning framework with teacher-student refinement. Therefore, experimental results show that proposed HAMLETNet achieves Raw Pitch Accuracy (RPA), of 89.47%, Raw Chroma Accuracy (RCA) of 90.24% and Overall Accuracy (OA) of 91.04% against competing methods. These results determine that harmonic-aware hierarchical learning greatly improves melody tracking accuracy, interpretability, and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Music source separation is a research field to separate a target sound source from a sound source consisting of multiple instruments. However, these conventional methods have a problem of degradation in separation performance at points where the time-frequency characteristics change rapidly. The problem has to do with the different shapes of the in-strument's sound on the spectrogram. In this paper, we focused on the relationship between musical instrument sounds andex-isting CNN to improve performance. As mentioned above, there are differences in the spectrograms of each instrumental sound, but the conventional methods ignore the differences and process them uniformly. For this reason, we considered that the appropriate convolution might not be applied to each instrument sound. Therefore, we thought that we could improve the separation performance by deforming the shape of thegazing region according to the local feature changes in the time-frequency space of the music component sounds. Specifically, we propose a method using Deformable Convolution (Deform-Conv) to follow the local features of each instrumental sound. Compared with the conventional method, the SDR (separation performance measure) of Vocals was improved by 0.4 pt, confirming the basic effectiveness of this method.

Abstract: Audio Large Language Models (Audio LLMs) enable human-like conversation about music, yet it is unclear if they are truly listening to the audio or just using textual reasoning, as recent benchmarks suggest. This paper investigates this issue by quantifying the contribution of each modality to a model’s output. We adapt the MM-SHAP framework, a performance-agnostic score based on Shapley values that quantifies the relative contribution of each modality to a model’s prediction. We evaluate two models on the MuChoMusic benchmark and find that the model with higher accuracy relies more on text to answer questions, but further inspection shows that even if the overall audio contribution is low, models can successfully localize key sound events, suggesting that audio is not entirely ignored. Abstract: From the past few years, smart music therapy systems have developed as a key tool for emotional regulation and cognitive rehabilitation. Abstract: We present Ta Ka Di Mi, an AI-powered mobile application designed to make rhythm training and Konnakkol-the South Indian art of vocal percussion-accessible to learners worldwide. Konnakkol is a centuries-old tradition where rhythmic syllables are recited to convey complex tala (time cycles or rhythmic frameworks), often serving as both a pedagogical tool and a performance art. While deeply rooted in South Indian classical music, rhythm itself is a universal language, integral to music, dance, speech, and movement across cultures. Despite this universality, learning Konnakkol has traditionally required close mentorship, consistent practice, and significant exposure to expert practitioners, making it less accessible outside specialized communities. Our system addresses these challenges by combining modern AI technologies with carefully curated pedagogical content to create an interactive, scalable, and userfriendly learning experience for global audiences. The application integrates automatic speech recognition, audio signal processing, and real-time feedback models to evaluate learners across multiple dimensions: rhythmic accuracy, pronunciation fidelity, and tempo consistency. Through gamified lessons, learners can progress from foundational exercises to advanced rhythmic improvisations, guided by structured modules designed in collaboration with renowned artist B.C. Manjunath. The AIdriven evaluation engine provides instant personalized feedback, enabling learners to refine their skills iteratively and at their own pace. In addition, the app incorporates adaptive learning strategies that track a user's growth over time and dynamically suggest exercises tailored to individual strengths and weaknesses. Our demo highlights the unique intersection of cultural heritage and modern AI, showcasing how domain-specific AI/ML techniques can be applied to music education in a globally meaningful way. Beyond traditional music training, Ta Ka Di Mi supports diverse use cases, including rhythm practice for dancers and instrumentalists, therapeutic interventions for speech and motor coordination, and cross-cultural exploration for learners worldwide who seek rhythm as a bridge across traditions. By blending ancient tradition with contemporary technology, the platform not only preserves and disseminates an important cultural practice but also reimagines how rhythm can be taught, experienced, and shared universally. Ultimately, Ta Ka Di Mi demonstrates how AI can democratize access to art forms, enhance pedagogy through personalized insights, and foster global community-building around rhythm. In bridging the gap between ancient tradition and modern technology, the system illustrates the potential of AI-powered education platforms to enrich cultural learning and inspire new creative possibilities across the world. Abstract: Puppet animation translates human bodily articulation into the mediated movement of an inanimate, jointed form. Artificial intelligence, through computer vision and generative modeling, offers new methods to analyze, simulate, and augment this translation, challenging traditional boundaries between human and algorithmic animation. This review examines how AI interprets and applies principles of bodily articulation to puppet animation, assesses the technical and artistic challenges of algorithmic animation systems, and identifies key research priorities for ethical and effective integration. A systematic search of interdisciplinary databases (2014–2025) identified studies applying AI to puppet or object animation. Included works were evaluated for technical validation and artistic relevance, with focus on movement translation and animator intent. AI motion capture accurately tracks human movement but struggles to map it to non-standard puppet joints (errors: 50–80mm). Generative animation models produce kinematically coherent motion but lack narrative intent and perceived weight, leading to low artistic acceptance for autonomous systems. AI augmentation tools, such as predictive movement assistance, showed higher practitioner acceptance (75%). A core "animation paradox" persists: AI manipulates kinematic chains effectively but fails to replicate the intentional, causal decision-making of a puppeteer. AI currently functions best as an analytical and assistive tool within the animation pipeline, not as a creative substitute. Future development must prioritize puppet-specific physical modeling, ethically curated animation datasets, and frameworks that uphold artistic agency. The integration of AI represents a triangulation of intelligences: the animator’s embodied skill, the algorithm’s analytical capacity, and the puppet’s performed expression

Abstract: Supervised machine learning for music information retrieval requires a large annotated training set, and is thus an expensive and time-consuming process. To circumvent this problem, we propose to train deep neural networks to perceive beats in musical recordings despite having little or no access to human annotations. The key idea is to train two fully convolutional networks in parallel, which we name “Zero-Note Samba” (ZeroNS): the first analyzes the percussive part of a musical piece whilst the second analyzes its non-percussive part. These networks learn a self-supervised pretext task of synchrony prediction (sync-pred), which simulates the ability of musicians to groove together when playing in the same band. Sync-pred encourages the two networks to return similar outputs if the underlying musical parts are synchronized, yet dissimilar outputs if the parts are out of sync. In practice, we obtain the instrumental parts from commercial recordings via an off-the-shelf source separation system: Spleeter. After self-supervised learning with sync-pred, ZeroNS produces a sparse output that resembles a beat detection function. When used in conjunction with a dynamic Bayesian network, ZeroNS surpasses the state of the art in unsupervised beat tracking. Furthermore, fine-tuning ZeroNS to a small set of labeled data (of the order of one to ten songs) matches the performance of a fully supervised network on 96 songs. Lastly, we show that pre-training a supervised model with sync-pred mitigates dataset bias and thus improves cross-dataset generalization, at no extra annotation cost.

Abstract: Musical style transfer seeks to reimagine a piece in a new stylistic domain while preserving its core musical identity, including melody, harmony, and rhythmic structure. Although recent deep learning advances have enabled progress in symbolic composition, timbre modelling, and neural audio generation, style transfer at the full audio level remains highly challenging. Existing methods commonly focus on symbolic transformations, isolated timbre conversion, or texture-based spectral manipulation, which limits their ability to achieve coherent genre-level transformation with realistic production characteristics. In particular, converting classical music into contemporary pop style requires simultaneously reshaping timbre, articulation, ornamentation, expressive dynamics, and modern production aesthetics without compromising musical fidelity. To address this problem, we present a two-stage deep learning framework for end-to-end classical-to-pop audio style transfer. The system first learns a genre-conditioned latent representation that captures multi-dimensional stylistic cues, and then integrates these features into a content-preserving transformation network for waveform synthesis. Harmonic–percussive decomposition, content-aligned losses, and a high-fidelity neural vocoder are incorporated to enhance clarity, rhythmic articulation, and expressive nuance. Across objective and subjective evaluations, our approach outperforms state-of-the-art symbolic, adversarial, and diffusion-based baselines in content preservation, style fidelity, perceptual quality, and Fréchet Audio Distance. Listening tests confirm that the transformed outputs maintain classical musical identity while rendering convincing pop timbre and production polish, establishing a strong foundation for creative music generation and cross-genre audio synthesis.

To ensure comparability, models were trained under identical conditions and evaluated quantitatively through loss, accuracy, and F1 score, as well as qualitatively through visual inspection using MuseScore. Abstract: Objective: Alzheimer's disease, a progressive neurodegenerative disorder, involves neural, genetic, and proteomic factors and impacts multiple cognitive and behavioral domains. Methods: Integrating missing data analysis, predictive modeling, multimodal data analysis, and explainable AI (XAI), we propose OPTIMUS, a predictive and explainable machine-learning framework, to unveil the many-to-many predictive pathways between multimodal input data and multivariate disease outcomes amidst missing values. Abstract: Dualisms are pervasive. The divisions between the rational mind, the physical body, and the external natural world have set the stage for the successes and failures of contemporary cognitive science and artificial intelligence.1 Advanced machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) systems have been developed to draw art and compose music. Many take these facts as calls for a radical shift in our values and turn to questions about AI ethics, rights, and personhood. While the discussion of agency and rights is not wrong in principle, it is a form of misdirection in the current circumstances. In this work, we propose a weakly supervised instance segmentation framework, Pixel-driven Weakly-supervised Instance Segmentation (PWISeg), to solve the occluded instrument localization with low-cost annotations. Specifically, We utilize the projection relationship between the bounding box and the surgical instrument mask as a supervision signal to train PWISeg to predict coarse masks of surgical instruments. Document Title: Explanation of Machine Learning Algorithms Used in Disease Detection, Such as Decision Trees and Neural Networks
Authors: N. Verma; T. Sharma; B. Kaur
Author Affiliations: ECE Department, Chandigarh University, Punjab, India; ECE Department, Chandigarh University, Punjab, India; ECE Department, Chandigarh University, Punjab, India
Publication Title: AI in Disease Detection: Advancements and Applications
Abstract:
Summary

Machine learning (ML) as a tool in disease diagnosis has come of age. However, in voluminous medical data, outclasses in analysis for digging out hidden patterns helpful for early detection. Current methodologies are effective, notwithstanding their limitations. This chapter thus presents a new approach – a “symphony of innovation” – by redefining disease detection to integrate the established methods with truly groundbreaking advancements systematically.

This symphony goes beyond established, well‐rehearsed routines of current ML practices. It introduces AI orchestration as a new conductor that would work in harmony with all other components involved in the disease detection workflow and adapt to the workflow. Picture a network of hospitals, each equipped with patient data as if playing a concert together. Federated learning is a way to train ML models on this “decentralized data,” unlocking its power for improved disease detection, especially in the case of rare or geographically isolated cases, while protecting patient privacy in the process – just like each hospital keeping its sheet music confidential. Another innovation in this symphony is active learning. Traditionally, training ML models require massive amounts of meticulously labeled data. Active learning algorithms act as a discerning conductor that identifies the most informative data points, reducing the need for manual labeling. This reduces not only the workload for medical professionals but also enables more efficient and targeted data collection.

Static models can become useless with a changing healthcare environment and patient populations. This symphony is introducing algorithms of continuous learning. These algorithms function much like an adaptable maestro, continuously ingesting new data streams and dynamically refining their predictions to provide perpetual improvement in disease detection accuracy from day one – to keep up with the evolving landscape in healthcare.

Generative adversarial networks will elevate the level of innovation further. Now, imagine having to compose a host of entirely new musical pieces – all those that would enhance the repertoire of an orchestra. GANs are capable of generating synthetic, false, anonymous patient data. In this way, researchers can train ML models on more extensive and more diverse datasets without being faced with questions about privacy, which is very useful in case of rare diseases with minimal real‐world data.

This symphony of innovation, however, does not end with technological progress but is also reflected in the harmonious concurrence of man's brain and machine expertise.

XAI techniques focused on the medical domain will turn out to be critical. As a model, such techniques are bound to provide transparent and interpretable explanations for their predictions, engendering trust and acceptance among medical professionals. Picture a program accompanying a complex musical piece that lets an audience – here, doctors – understand the intricacies of the performance. AI‐empowered clinical decision support systems are real‐time companions for a doctor in arriving at a diagnosis and instituting treatment. They study patient data, offer possible diagnoses, and suggest treatment options. Picture an AI assistant that can provide insights in real‐time and help doctors make more learned decisions about the clinical history of their patients. The ultimate goal of this symphony is personalized medicine. Armed with a patient's case history, genomic data, and data related to lifestyle habits, AI can predict their individual disease risk and hence help in the formulation of targeted interventions. Proactive healthcare – prevention before manifestation of the actual disease – is within reach.

This vision in disease detection goes beyond the conventionally adopted methods in disease detection. With AI orchestration, human‐machine collaboration, and ethical concerns, we can unleash the promise of healthcare with very early diagnoses and a treatment regime that makes a difference – leading to a genuinely healthful future for all. Such a symphony of innovation is taking to the streets and on course to rewrite the score relating to disease detection; every advancement within its repertoire seems to act as a note of hope in this fight for global health.

Publication Title: 2025 3rd International Conference on Intelligent Cyber Physical Systems and Internet of Things (ICoICI)
Abstract: With the rapid growth of telescopic technologies and space exploration missions, the amount of astronomical data captured as images, videos, and audio from satellites has increased significantly. Manually classifying such large datasets is no longer practical, making automated solutions necessary. Most existing studies focus on limited binary or three-class classification tasks, which leaves a gap in models that can handle a wider range of celestial objectsIn this paper, we propose a novel five-class ensemble deep learning model for the classification of planets, comets, asteroids, black holes, and constellations. Our method combines the advantages of VGG19, which is renowned for its effective layered feature extraction, with ResNet101V2, which is known for its residual connections and capacity to train deep networks effectively. The ensemble achieves a validation accuracy of 95.7% and a training accuracy of 99.7% using a soft voting technique. These outcomes show how well the model performs in classifying multi-class astronomical images and offer a potent instrument for enhancing automated analysis in space exploration.

Document Title: Variational Autoencoders for Polyphonic Music Interpolation
Authors: P. L. Diéguez; V. -W. Soo
Author Affiliations: Institute of Information Systems and Applications, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan; Department of Computer Science, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan
Publication Title: 2020 International Conference on Technologies and Applications of Artificial Intelligence (TAAI)
Abstract: This paper aims to use machine learning techniques to solve the novel problem of music interpolation composition. Two models based on Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) are proposed to generate a suitable polyphonic harmonic bridge between two given songs, smoothly changing the pitches and dynamics of the interpolation. The interpolations generated by the first model surpass a Random data baseline and a bidirectional LSTM approach and its performance is comparable to the current state-of-the-art. The novel architecture of the second model outperforms the state-of-the-art interpolation approaches in terms of reconstruction loss by using an additional neural network for direct estimation of the interpolation encoded vector. Furthermore, the Hsinchu Interpolation MIDI Dataset was created, making both models proposed in this paper more efficient than previous approaches in the literature in terms of computational and time requirements during training. Finally, a subjective evaluation was done in order to ensure the validity of the metric-based results.
Author Keywords: Polyphonic Music Composition;VAE;Music Interpolation
